title: Brugada Syndrome
oneLiner: Inherited sodium channelopathy causing coved ST elevation in V1–V3 and risk of sudden cardiac death from ventricular fibrillation; typically young males, often first triggered by fever.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute anterior STEMI** – ST elevation in V1–V3 mimics Brugada, but pain + dynamic troponin change = treat as STEMI first  
  - *Single finding:* Chest pain + >1 mm ST elevation in contiguous leads with reciprocal depression  
- **Hyperkalaemia / hypercalcaemia** – Can produce a Brugada-like pattern  
  - *Single finding:* Potassium >6.0 mmol/L or corrected calcium >2.8 mmol/L on blood gas – must be corrected before attributing ECG to Brugada  
- **Active ventricular arrhythmias (VF / polymorphic VT) in ED** – Often the *presentation*; treat per ALS immediately  
  - *Single finding:* Shockable rhythm on monitor – defibrillate, do not wait for ECG interpretation
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Syncope (especially at rest, during sleep, or with fever)  
  - Palpitations / presyncope  
  - Nocturnal agonal respiration (suggests arrhythmia during sleep)  
  - Family history of unexplained sudden cardiac death in <40-year-old  
  - Often asymptomatic – discovered incidentally on ECG for other reasons  
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Usually normal cardiac exam  
  - May have fever (temperature >38°C) – a common trigger for unmasking Brugada pattern
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute anterior STEMI** – chest pain, dynamic troponin rise, reciprocal changes in inferior leads  
- **Benign early repolarisation (BER)** – no symptoms, no family history, ST elevation is concave, J-point notching in lateral leads  
- **Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC)** – epsilon wave in V1, T-wave inversion beyond V1–V3, RV structural abnormalities on echo/MRI  
- **Pericarditis** – widespread concave ST elevation, PR depression, pericardial rub, pain worse lying flat  
- **Pulmonary embolism** – right heart strain (S1Q3T3), tachycardia, hypoxia, raised D-dimer  
- **Splitter:** Fever-induced ECG changes → strongly favours Brugada; chest pain + troponin rise → STEMI
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - 12-lead ECG – look for Type 1 Brugada pattern: coved ST elevation ≥2 mm in ≥1 lead among V1–V3 followed by negative T wave (may require placement of leads V1–V2 one or two intercostal spaces higher)  
  - Monitor for arrhythmias – connect to cardiac monitor immediately  
  - Check temperature – fever unmasks Brugada  
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es (especially K+, Ca²⁺), troponin, D-dimer (if PE suspected)  
  - Blood cultures if febrile  
  - Genetic testing for SCN5A mutation (not urgent; done by cardiology clinic)  
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transthoracic echocardiogram – exclude structural heart disease (normal in Brugada)  
  - **Second-line:** Cardiac MRI if suspicion of ARVC or sarcoidosis  
- **Special tests:**
  - Drug challenge (ajmaline, flecainide, or procainamide) – only in cardiology/EP department with resuscitation equipment, NOT on ward  
  - Provocative test with high-lead ECG (V1–V2 placed in 2nd and 3rd intercostal spaces)  
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **Consensus Criteria:** Type 1 Brugada pattern in ≥1 right precordial lead (V1–V3), either spontaneous or after drug challenge, PLUS ≥1 of: documented VF/polymorphic VT, syncope of suspected arrhythmic cause, nocturnal agonal respiration, family history of sudden cardiac death at <45 years, or family history of Type 1 ECG  
- **Never-miss test:** ECG in any syncope, palpitations, or family history of sudden death – ECG is the single investigation whose omission is catastrophic
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable (VF/pVT) → defibrillate per ALS algorithm; amiodarone if refractory (but see **most dangerous dose error** below)  
  - If unstable polymorphic VT → electrical cardioversion  
  - Correct fever aggressively – paracetamol + tepid sponging; source control (sepsis?)  
  - Stop any culprit drugs (e.g., class IC antiarrhythmics, tricyclic antidepressants, lithium) – check **BrugadaDrugs.org**  
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Admit to telemetry or coronary care unit  
  - Continuous ECG monitoring for arrhythmias  
  - Cardiology consult for risk stratification (EP study, ICD decision)  
  - Avoid drugs that prolong QT or block sodium channels – check **www.brugadadrugs.org** for every new prescription  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **ICD implant** – indicated for survivors of cardiac arrest, syncope with spontaneous Type 1 pattern, or documented VF  
  - Quinidine may be used as an ICD adjunct or in patients who decline ICD (dose: [check local policy])  
  - Lifestyle advice: avoid fever (vaccinate, early antipyretics), avoid alcohol excess, avoid cocaine, avoid high-intensity exercise if symptomatic  
  - Family screening (first-degree relatives) – ECG + genetic counselling  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Maintenance IV fluids if septic (Hartmann’s or Plasmalyte – no specific restriction)  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if not bleeding) – no contraindication unless on anticoagulation  
  - **Diet:** Normal oral intake  
  - **Observation:** Continuous cardiac monitoring; check temperature 4-hourly; NEWS2 hourly initially
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents for rhythm control:**  
  - Quinidine (if used) – 200–400 mg PO TDS (start low, titrate)[check local policy] – only on cardiology advice  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Isoproterenol (IV infusion) – used in acute electrical storm (dose: [check local policy]) – only in ICU/CCU  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - **Absolute contraindication:** Class IC antiarrhythmics (flecainide, propafenone), verapamil, tricyclic antidepressants, lithium, certain antipsychotics (check list)  
  - Fever – untreated fever can precipitate VF  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific adjustment for quinidine, but check ECG for QTc prolongation  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving flecainide to a Brugada patient** – can cause refractory VF/death  
  - **Giving IV amiodarone in acute arrhythmia** – amiodarone is a sodium channel blocker and may worsen the Brugada pattern; use only if no alternative per cardiology
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Ventricular fibrillation, polymorphic VT, sudden cardiac death (often first presentation)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - ICD-related: infection, lead failure, inappropriate shocks (e.g., for AF, sinus tachycardia)  
  - Psychosocial: anxiety, fear of arrhythmia, activity restriction  
  - Risk of cardiac arrest if ICD not implanted in high-risk patients
sections.Prognosis:
- Asymptomatic with incidental Type 1 pattern and no family history → low risk (1–3% risk of arrhythmia over 10 years)  
- Symptomatic (syncope or cardiac arrest) → high risk of recurrence; ICD reduces mortality to <5% at 5 years
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check temperature** in any patient with a Brugada-pattern ECG – fever can change a benign Type 2/3 into a dangerous Type 1  
- **Use BrugadaDrugs.org** every time you prescribe a new medication (including OTCs like antihistamines)  
- **Refer all patients with a Type 1 pattern** (even if asymptomatic) to cardiology/EP for risk stratification and family counselling
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not ignore a Brugada-pattern ECG** – even in an asymptomatic patient; it warrants cardiology review  
- **Do not give IV amiodarone for AF or VT in a Brugada patient** without cardiology approval – it can worsen the arrhythmia  
- **Do not discharge a patient with syncope and a Brugada ECG** without in-hospital telemetry and cardiology assessment (high risk of lethal arrhythmia)
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Any further syncope, presyncope, or palpitations  
  - Fever that does not respond to antipyretics (risk of unmasking arrhythmia)  
  - Chest pain or shortness of breath (to rule out STEMI)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Cardiology clinic within 2 weeks for EP evaluation and consideration of drug challenge / ICD  
  - Genetic counselling referral for patient and first-degree relatives
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call crash team / 2222:**  
  - Cardiac arrest, sustained VT/VF  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising trend  
  - GCS <13  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L  
- **Call cardiology reg (urgently):**  
  - New syncope with spontaneous Type 1 ECG  
  - Refractory arrhythmia (electrical storm)  
  - Fever >38.5°C in a known Brugada patient – need aggressive cooling and monitoring  
- **Call ICU if:**  
  - Haemodynamic instability despite correction of arrhythmia  
  - Need for vasopressors or isoproterenol infusion

rawMarkdown:
## Brugada Syndrome  
**One-liner:** Inherited sodium channelopathy causing coved ST elevation in V1–V3 and risk of sudden cardiac death from ventricular fibrillation; typically young males, often first triggered by fever.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute anterior STEMI** – ST elevation in V1–V3 mimics Brugada, but pain + dynamic troponin change = treat as STEMI first  
  - *Single finding:* Chest pain + >1 mm ST elevation in contiguous leads with reciprocal depression  
- **Hyperkalaemia / hypercalcaemia** – Can produce a Brugada-like pattern  
  - *Single finding:* Potassium >6.0 mmol/L or corrected calcium >2.8 mmol/L on blood gas – must be corrected before attributing ECG to Brugada  
- **Active ventricular arrhythmias (VF / polymorphic VT) in ED** – Often the *presentation*; treat per ALS immediately  
  - *Single finding:* Shockable rhythm on monitor – defibrillate, do not wait for ECG interpretation

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Syncope (especially at rest, during sleep, or with fever)  
  - Palpitations / presyncope  
  - Nocturnal agonal respiration (suggests arrhythmia during sleep)  
  - Family history of unexplained sudden cardiac death in <40-year-old  
  - Often asymptomatic – discovered incidentally on ECG for other reasons  
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Usually normal cardiac exam  
  - May have fever (temperature >38°C) – a common trigger for unmasking Brugada pattern

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute anterior STEMI** – chest pain, dynamic troponin rise, reciprocal changes in inferior leads  
- **Benign early repolarisation (BER)** – no symptoms, no family history, ST elevation is concave, J-point notching in lateral leads  
- **Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC)** – epsilon wave in V1, T-wave inversion beyond V1–V3, RV structural abnormalities on echo/MRI  
- **Pericarditis** – widespread concave ST elevation, PR depression, pericardial rub, pain worse lying flat  
- **Pulmonary embolism** – right heart strain (S1Q3T3), tachycardia, hypoxia, raised D-dimer  
- **Splitter:** Fever-induced ECG changes → strongly favours Brugada; chest pain + troponin rise → STEMI

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - 12-lead ECG – look for Type 1 Brugada pattern: coved ST elevation ≥2 mm in ≥1 lead among V1–V3 followed by negative T wave (may require placement of leads V1–V2 one or two intercostal spaces higher)  
  - Monitor for arrhythmias – connect to cardiac monitor immediately  
  - Check temperature – fever unmasks Brugada  
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es (especially K+, Ca²⁺), troponin, D-dimer (if PE suspected)  
  - Blood cultures if febrile  
  - Genetic testing for SCN5A mutation (not urgent; done by cardiology clinic)  
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Transthoracic echocardiogram – exclude structural heart disease (normal in Brugada)  
  - **Second-line:** Cardiac MRI if suspicion of ARVC or sarcoidosis  
- **Special tests:**
  - Drug challenge (ajmaline, flecainide, or procainamide) – only in cardiology/EP department with resuscitation equipment, NOT on ward  
  - Provocative test with high-lead ECG (V1–V2 placed in 2nd and 3rd intercostal spaces)  
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **Consensus Criteria:** Type 1 Brugada pattern in ≥1 right precordial lead (V1–V3), either spontaneous or after drug challenge, PLUS ≥1 of: documented VF/polymorphic VT, syncope of suspected arrhythmic cause, nocturnal agonal respiration, family history of sudden cardiac death at <45 years, or family history of Type 1 ECG  
- **Never-miss test:** ECG in any syncope, palpitations, or family history of sudden death – ECG is the single investigation whose omission is catastrophic

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If haemodynamically unstable (VF/pVT) → defibrillate per ALS algorithm; amiodarone if refractory (but see **most dangerous dose error** below)  
  - If unstable polymorphic VT → electrical cardioversion  
  - Correct fever aggressively – paracetamol + tepid sponging; source control (sepsis?)  
  - Stop any culprit drugs (e.g., class IC antiarrhythmics, tricyclic antidepressants, lithium) – check **BrugadaDrugs.org**  
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Admit to telemetry or coronary care unit  
  - Continuous ECG monitoring for arrhythmias  
  - Cardiology consult for risk stratification (EP study, ICD decision)  
  - Avoid drugs that prolong QT or block sodium channels – check **www.brugadadrugs.org** for every new prescription  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **ICD implant** – indicated for survivors of cardiac arrest, syncope with spontaneous Type 1 pattern, or documented VF  
  - Quinidine may be used as an ICD adjunct or in patients who decline ICD (dose: [check local policy])  
  - Lifestyle advice: avoid fever (vaccinate, early antipyretics), avoid alcohol excess, avoid cocaine, avoid high-intensity exercise if symptomatic  
  - Family screening (first-degree relatives) – ECG + genetic counselling  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** Maintenance IV fluids if septic (Hartmann’s or Plasmalyte – no specific restriction)  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if not bleeding) – no contraindication unless on anticoagulation  
  - **Diet:** Normal oral intake  
  - **Observation:** Continuous cardiac monitoring; check temperature 4-hourly; NEWS2 hourly initially

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents for rhythm control:**  
  - Quinidine (if used) – 200–400 mg PO TDS (start low, titrate)[check local policy] – only on cardiology advice  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Isoproterenol (IV infusion) – used in acute electrical storm (dose: [check local policy]) – only in ICU/CCU  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - **Absolute contraindication:** Class IC antiarrhythmics (flecainide, propafenone), verapamil, tricyclic antidepressants, lithium, certain antipsychotics (check list)  
  - Fever – untreated fever can precipitate VF  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific adjustment for quinidine, but check ECG for QTc prolongation  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving flecainide to a Brugada patient** – can cause refractory VF/death  
  - **Giving IV amiodarone in acute arrhythmia** – amiodarone is a sodium channel blocker and may worsen the Brugada pattern; use only if no alternative per cardiology

### Complications
- **Acute:** Ventricular fibrillation, polymorphic VT, sudden cardiac death (often first presentation)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - ICD-related: infection, lead failure, inappropriate shocks (e.g., for AF, sinus tachycardia)  
  - Psychosocial: anxiety, fear of arrhythmia, activity restriction  
  - Risk of cardiac arrest if ICD not implanted in high-risk patients

### Prognosis
- Asymptomatic with incidental Type 1 pattern and no family history → low risk (1–3% risk of arrhythmia over 10 years)  
- Symptomatic (syncope or cardiac arrest) → high risk of recurrence; ICD reduces mortality to <5% at 5 years

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check temperature** in any patient with a Brugada-pattern ECG – fever can change a benign Type 2/3 into a dangerous Type 1  
- **Use BrugadaDrugs.org** every time you prescribe a new medication (including OTCs like antihistamines)  
- **Refer all patients with a Type 1 pattern** (even if asymptomatic) to cardiology/EP for risk stratification and family counselling

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not ignore a Brugada-pattern ECG** – even in an asymptomatic patient; it warrants cardiology review  
- **Do not give IV amiodarone for AF or VT in a Brugada patient** without cardiology approval – it can worsen the arrhythmia  
- **Do not discharge a patient with syncope and a Brugada ECG** without in-hospital telemetry and cardiology assessment (high risk of lethal arrhythmia)

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Any further syncope, presyncope, or palpitations  
  - Fever that does not respond to antipyretics (risk of unmasking arrhythmia)  
  - Chest pain or shortness of breath (to rule out STEMI)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Cardiology clinic within 2 weeks for EP evaluation and consideration of drug challenge / ICD  
  - Genetic counselling referral for patient and first-degree relatives

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call crash team / 2222:**  
  - Cardiac arrest, sustained VT/VF  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising trend  
  - GCS <13  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L  
- **Call cardiology reg (urgently):**  
  - New syncope with spontaneous Type 1 ECG  
  - Refractory arrhythmia (electrical storm)  
  - Fever >38.5°C in a known Brugada patient – need aggressive cooling and monitoring  
- **Call ICU if:**  
  - Haemodynamic instability despite correction of arrhythmia  
  - Need for vasopressors or isoproterenol infusion
